claim: Token efficiency (tokens per unit of work accomplished) matters more than wall-clock execution time for evaluating model improvements; GPT-5 to 5.1 gained performance while reducing tokens used
actionable: Optimize for token efficiency in evals; measure model versions on a 2D plot of tokens vs. performance to identify true gains
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: Post-training RL infrastructure has way more moving parts than pre-training (per-task grading setups, external partnerships) making it harder to debug and troubleshoot at scale
actionable: Budget extra debugging time and infrastructure complexity when scaling RL post-training; have clear ownership of task definitions and grading logic
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: RLHF and RLVR are both policy gradient methods; the distinction is the input data quality (human preference vs. verifiable answers like math), not the optimization algorithm
actionable: When comparing RL methods, evaluate signal trustworthiness (can you verify the answer is correct?) rather than focusing on gradient variance details
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: Codex/Claude Code changes workflow fundamentally—40 minutes of design doc writing replaced by 15 minutes of agent execution, creating temporal gaps that disrupt engineering rhythm
actionable: Plan for workflow disruption when deploying coding agents; engineers will need to develop new habits for the 15-minute execution windows between long design phases
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: Interruptability and chain-of-thought transparency (showing the agent's reasoning, allowing the user to interrupt and steer) is a new interaction paradigm users value highly
actionable: Design agents to surface intermediate reasoning steps and accept mid-task corrections; enable users to press escape and redirect execution
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: Personality toggles (Anton vs. Clippy) are a real differentiator that users care about; some users want tools that quietly work without warmth, others prefer friendly interaction
actionable: Allow users to customize model personality and tone via toggles or custom instructions to match their workflow preferences
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: GRPO (from DeepSeek Math) is underappreciated; it enables reliable reward signals (math verification) rather than fallible human preference signals, shifting post-training from opinion to verification
actionable: Prefer post-training data where the reward signal is verifiable (code tests pass, math is correct) over subjective human preference scoring
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI

claim: Education systems are not producing enough people skilled in both distributed systems and ML research; this dual skill set is critical for pushing the frontier of scaling RL
actionable: If you are a student or educator, invest in learning both distributed systems/engineering and ML research; this combination is in high demand
source_title: [State of Post-Training] From GPT-4.1 to 5.1: RLVR, Agent & Token Efficiency — Josh McGrath, OpenAI
